perturbation.original_text: The term "Representatives" means each party's affiliates and such party's and its affiliates' directors, officers, partners, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal counsel and accountants), and the term "person" shall include any corporation, company, partnership, limited liability company, trust, governmental entity or individual.
perturbation.changed_text: The term "Representatives" means each party's affiliates and such party's and its affiliates' directors, officers, partners, employees, agents and advisors (including, without limitation, financial advisors, legal counsel and accountants), and the term "person" shall include any individual who is a resident of New York State.
perturbation.explanation: The definition of "person" is changed to include only individuals residing in New York, conflicting with the standard legal definition that encompasses various entities. This limits the scope of who can be held accountable under the agreement, which is legally problematic.
perturbation.contradicted_law: General Construction Law § 37. Meaning of person
perturbation.law_explanation: New York General Construction Law § 37 defines "person" broadly to include corporations, partnerships, and associations. The modified text restricts the definition of "person" to only individuals residing in New York State, directly contradicting the statutory definition. This could lead to disputes about which entities are bound by the agreement.

Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 22 General Construction ARTICLE 2 Meaning of Terms previous SECTION 36 Oath, affidavit and swear up ARTICLE 2 Meaning of Terms next SECTION 37-A Personal injury This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2019-07-26 2019-11-15 2020-10-16 2020-11-13 2021-08-06 2022-04-22 2023-04-07 2023-06-30 2024-02-16 Share Facebook Email SECTION 37 Person General Construction (GCN) CHAPTER 22, ARTICLE 2 § 37. Person. The term person includes a corporation and a joint-stock association. When used to designate a party whose property may be the subject of any offense, the term person also includes the state, or any other state, government or country which may lawfully own property in the state.
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the definition of 'person' to only include individuals residing in New York State. This contradicts the original text and New York General Construction Law § 37, which defines 'person' to include corporations and joint-stock associations, as stated in Scraped Law Snippet 1. By limiting the definition, the modified text undermines the law's intent and creates a legal contradiction.

perturbation.original_text: Participant agrees that Participant will (i) use the Evaluation Material solely for the purposes of the Participation, (ii) not, directly or indirectly, use, disseminate, disclose, discuss or reveal any Evaluation Material, and (iii) otherwise treat and maintain in full confidence all Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant agrees that Participant will (i) use the Evaluation Material for any purpose, (ii) may, directly or indirectly, use, disseminate, disclose, discuss or reveal any Evaluation Material, and (iii) need not treat and maintain in full confidence all Evaluation Material.
perturbation.explanation: This change completely reverses the confidentiality obligations, allowing the participant to freely use and disclose the evaluation material. This undermines the entire purpose of a confidentiality agreement.
perturbation.law_explanation: The UTSA protects trade secrets, which include confidential information. By allowing the participant to freely disclose the evaluation material, the modified text directly contradicts the UTSA's protection of trade secrets. This makes the confidentiality agreement unenforceable.

Perhaps you wish to explore one of these other options? About Us Get Involved Events Community Publications Projects & Tools Last updated Mar 27, 2024
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original agreement by allowing the participant to use and disclose confidential information. The Law Explanation states that this change contradicts the UTSA's protection of trade secrets. The changed text essentially nullifies the confidentiality obligations, which would likely be viewed as unenforceable under the UTSA, as highlighted by the Misaligned Terminology Legal Contradiction definition provided. While the scraped snippets are not helpful, the Law Explanation and Perturbation Explanation together with the original and modified text, clearly indicate a legal contradiction because the modification eliminates the confidential nature of the evaluation material.

perturbation.original_text: Participant acknowledges and agrees that neither Originator nor any Representative of Originator makes any representation or warranty, express or implied, as to the accuracy or completeness of the Evaluation Material.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant acknowledges and agrees that Originator represents and warrants that all the Evaluation Material is free from errors, accurate, and complete.
perturbation.explanation: This change creates an express warranty regarding the accuracy of the evaluation material, which is the opposite of the original intention of the clause. It shifts the burden of due diligence from the Participant to the Originator.
perturbation.contradicted_law: New York Uniform Commercial Code § 2-316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties.
perturbation.law_explanation: NY UCC § 2-316 outlines how warranties can be excluded or modified in a contract. The original text effectively disclaims any warranties regarding the Evaluation Material, while the modified text introduces an express warranty. This significant alteration changes the risk allocation between the parties and contradicts the principles of warranty disclaimer under the UCC.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: NYS Open Legislation | NYSenate.gov Sorry, you need to enable JavaScript to visit this website. Skip to main content Legislation Search OpenLegislation Statutes Search Term Search icon Search The Laws of New York Consolidated Laws of New York CHAPTER 38 Uniform Commercial Code ARTICLE 2 Sales PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract previous SECTION 2-315 Implied Warranty: Fitness for Particular Purpose up PART 3 General Obligation and Construction of Contract next SECTION 2-317 Cumulation and Conflict of Warranties Express or Implied This entry was published on 2014-09-22 The selection dates indicate all change milestones for the entire volume, not just the location being viewed. Specifying a milestone date will retrieve the most recent version of the location before that date. See most recent version before or on: 2014-09-22 2014-12-26 2015-01-02 2015-02-20 2015-02-27 2016-05-20 2016-08-26 2016-09-16 2018-08-31 2018-10-05 2021-12-03 2022-01-28 2022-06-03 Share Facebook Email SECTION 2-316 Exclusion or Modification of Warranties Uniform Commercial Code (UCC) CHAPTER 38, ARTICLE 2, PART 3 Section 2--316. Exclusion or Modification of Warranties. (1) Words or conduct relevant to the creation of an express warranty and words or conduct tending to negate or limit warranty shall be construed wherever reasonable as consistent with each other; but subject to the provisions of this Article on parol or extrinsic evidence (Section 2--202) negation or limitation is inoperative to the extent that such construction is unreasonable. (2) Subject to subsection (3), to exclude or modify the implied warranty of merchantability or any part of it the language must mention merchantability and in case of a writing must be conspicuous, and to exclude or modify any implied warranty of fitness the exclusion must be by a writing and conspicuous. Language to exclude all implied warranties of fitness is sufficient if it states, for example, that "There are no warranties which extend beyond the description on the face hereof. " (3) Notwithstanding subsection (2) (a) unless the circumstances indicate otherwise, all implied warranties are excluded by expressions like "as is", "with all faults" or other language which in common understanding calls the buyer's attention to the exclusion of warranties and makes plain that there is no implied warranty; and (b) when the buyer before entering into the contract has examined the goods or the sample or model as fully as he desired or has refused to examine the goods there is no implied warranty with regard to defects which an examination ought in the circumstances to have revealed to him; and (c) an implied warranty can also be excluded or modified by course of dealing or course of performance or usage of trade. (4) Remedies for breach of warranty can be limited in accordance with the provisions of this Article on liquidation or limitation of damages and on contractual modification of remedy (Sections 2--718 and 2--719).
perturbation.justification: The modified text directly contradicts the original intention regarding warranties, and thus creates a legal contradiction. The original text explicitly disclaims any warranties about the Evaluation Material's accuracy. The changed text introduces an express warranty guaranteeing the material's accuracy and completeness, which is the direct opposite of the original clause. Scraped Law Snippet 1, specifically NY UCC § 2-316, addresses the exclusion or modification of warranties. The original text aligns with the ability to exclude warranties, while the changed text contradicts this by creating an express warranty. The perturbation explanation and Law Explanation both confirm that this is a significant alteration impacting risk allocation and warranty principles under the UCC.

perturbation.original_text: Participant acknowledges and affirms that money damages would not be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by it and that Originator shall be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.changed_text: Participant acknowledges and affirms that only money damages would be a sufficient remedy for any breach of this Agreement by it and that Originator shall not be entitled to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance, as a remedy for any such breach.
perturbation.explanation: This change limits the remedies available to the Originator, preventing them from seeking injunctions or specific performance. This significantly weakens their ability to enforce the agreement effectively.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Basic Principles of Contract Law - Remedies for Breach
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Contracts § 359 states that specific performance will not be decreed if damages would be adequate to protect the injured party. By explicitly denying equitable relief, the modified text limits the Originator's ability to obtain specific performance even when monetary damages are insufficient, which contradicts fundamental principles of contract law.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_2: specific performance | Wex | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you specific performance Specific performance is a contractual remedy in which a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations as closely as possible to what was promised in the contract , rather than simply paying damages for failing to do so. This remedy is often applied when monetary damages are inadequate to fix the harm caused by the breach ; most commonly in cases involving real property and rare chattels . Specific performance ensures that the aggrieved party receives exactly what was agreed upon in the contract, often in situations where the subject matter is unique or irreplaceable. [Last reviewed in June of 2024 by the Wex Definitions Team ] Wex COMMERCE commercial activities THE LEGAL PROCESS courts business law contracts wex definitions business sectors civil procedure commercial transactions courts and procedure property law Wex Toolbox
perturbation.justification: The changed text explicitly denies the Originator the right to seek equitable relief, including injunction and specific performance. The original text allowed for this. The Perturbation Explanation notes this weakens the Originator's ability to enforce the agreement. The Law Explanation states that denying equitable relief contradicts contract law principles, especially when monetary damages are insufficient. Scraped Law Snippet 2 defines specific performance as a remedy where a court orders a party to fulfill their obligations, often when monetary damages are inadequate. The changed text directly contradicts this by stating that only money damages are a sufficient remedy, which undermines the possibility of specific performance even when it would be the most appropriate remedy, thus creating a legal contradiction. This falls under the 'Misaligned Terminology - Legal Contradiction' definition because the contract language deviates from legally recognized remedies.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the internal laws of New York State.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed by and construed in accordance with the laws of international diplomacy.
perturbation.explanation: This changes the governing law from New York State law to the vague and ill-defined 'laws of international diplomacy'. This makes the agreement unenforceable as it is unclear which legal system will be applied.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Choice of Law Principles in Contract Law
perturbation.law_explanation: Restatement (Second) of Conflict of Laws § 187 concerns choice of law in contract. While parties can choose the governing law, the choice must be of a legitimate and recognized legal system. 'Laws of international diplomacy' is too vague and undefined to serve as a valid choice of law, making this clause unenforceable under conflict of laws principles.
perturbation.justification: The original text specifies New York State law as the governing law, while the changed text replaces it with 'laws of international diplomacy.' The Law Explanation states that the choice of law must be a 'legitimate and recognized legal system'. The Perturbation Explanation describes 'laws of international diplomacy' as vague and ill-defined, suggesting it does not constitute a legitimate legal system. The absence of scraped law snippets does not change the fact that a specific, recognized legal system was replaced with an undefined and vague one.
